body: It would also be possible to add a new (and maybe even default) feature configuration that creates a pure Rust build instead. With some directions I could certainly contribute it.

I just installed wsl with `wsl --install`, then installed Rust with Rustup, then tried to install Starship.
So I have as much of a default WSL system as you can get.

I ran into this issue and did 2 things to fix it
1. `sudo apt install build-essential`
2. `sudo apt install cmake`

The first because it complained `cc` linker was not found.
Then I got the "failed to run custom build command for libz-ng-sys v1.1.9" error, which was solved by installing `cmake`

Yes, i already knew about that approach.
But it's actually a completely different setup, since you're using linux under the hood.
The goal here is to have a bare minimum setup to build with rust on Windows.
Building rust on windows is either possible if you install the default windows tools, but you'd then have to install the msvc toolchain for it as well.
Using the windows-gnu toolchain removes that dependency, but as I said, it would not build anymore by just using the initial cli prompt.
Adding the flags seemed to have resolved the issue, so it is a thing of possibility.

I got the compilation working with everything by doing
```sh
pacman -S mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-gcc mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-cmake make
```
on MSYS2 shell, so installing ucrt64 CMake & GCC, then MSYS Make (you can replace ucrt64 with mingw64 or other). Just make sure the MSYS CMake is uninstalled.

yeah, that not what my issue is about though.
While configuring rustup on my windows machine I can just say I want to use windows-gnu and a lot of stuff is compilable without any problem. I do not need the whole mingw or msvc setup. I just say use windows-gnu.
When trying to build the latest locked 1.19.0 it won't even build with the command in the OP.
if it is any help, here's my current problem:

The fix for that, I believe, was commit 50a6b846045899c88d95b063a6b939a6dcb72a5c which is still unreleased. 
To validate it, you could try to build from source with `cargo install --git https://github.com/starship/starship` and share your findings here maybe? Thanks for giving it a shot.

I think it would really help if you would also publish the exact command-line that was run, not only the output.

With that said, as long as the `sha1-asm` issue isn't visible anymore, it looks good to me.
In Rust, even though there is only a high chance that something will build with `cargo install [--locked]`, even when there is no semver related issues, one might still run into C-toolchain dependencies missing, which seems to be the case here now.

To make this work without running into C-toolchain issues, `starship` provides compatibility options.

`cargo install --git https://github.com/starship/starship --no-default-features --features battery,notify` should build, and maybe using `--features battery,notify,gix-faster` will also work.

Yeah, should've done that.
But that last error message is exactly was i recieved after running your mentioned command:
`cargo install --git https://github.com/starship/starship --no-default-features --features=battery,notify,gix-faster`

I do remember it working as stated in the initial post and rust-lang/libz-sys#135

`cargo install --git https://github.com/starship/starship --no-default-features --features=battery,notify` should really be the one that works as it avoids all of the C-toolchain, at least from the side of `zlib`. If that's not working, try stripping features until it does - I'd expect certain features to possibly depend on bindings to C or C libraries which might come with portability issues.
